courses.title: Smart Manufacturing & Digital Twin
courses.summary: Industry 4.0 without the brochure: data architectures, digital-twin levels, simulation, and predictive analytics — evaluated with an engineer's skepticism and a maintenance career's use cases.
taught_from: NPTEL: Industry 4.0 and Industrial Internet of Things-family courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: The technology cluster named and sorted: connectivity, data, analytics, autonomy. Maturity models used as maps, not scorecards. Separating what is deployed from what is marketed.
preview: Sort connectivity, data, analytics, autonomy: which layer fails first in practice?
Deployed versus marketed: name one test that separates them.

lessons.scope: From sensor to historian to lake: the data pipeline with its quality gates. Context — asset hierarchies and event framing — as the expensive part. Why analytics projects die of data preparation.
preview: Why does context (hierarchy, event framing) cost more than storage?
Where do analytics projects actually die, and why?

lessons.t: MES and the manufacturing software stack
lessons.scope: The ISA-95 level picture: sensors and control below, ERP above, and manufacturing execution systems holding the middle. MES functions — scheduling, dispatching, genealogy and traceability, quality records — and where each earns its keep. The integration seams where smart-manufacturing projects actually fail.
preview: What does MES answer that neither SCADA nor ERP can?
Why is product genealogy a plant-floor function rather than an ERP report?

lessons.scope: Descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive twins distinguished by what they can answer. Asset versus process twins. An honest inventory of what each level costs to build and keep true.
preview: Distinguish the four twin levels by the question each answers.
What does keeping a twin true cost, ongoing?

lessons.scope: Discrete-event simulation for lines and buffers; validation against measured cycle data. Experiments on the model instead of the plant. The packaging line simulated for a debottlenecking decision.
preview: What validates a discrete-event model before you trust its experiments?
Why simulate the buffer sizes rather than argue about them?

lessons.scope: Feature extraction from vibration and process data; models from thresholds to learning methods in outline. Validation against labeled failures — the scarce commodity. Where analytics augments and where it hallucinates.
preview: Why are labeled failures the scarce commodity of PdM analytics?
What can a threshold do that a learned model cannot — and vice versa?

lessons.scope: Cameras, lighting, and defect detection on moving product. Classical vision versus learned models. False-reject economics on a high-speed line.
preview: Why does lighting beat algorithms in machine vision?
What does a false reject cost on a high-speed line?

lessons.scope: Scan-to-print for obsolete spares; digital part inventories. Qualification questions for load-bearing prints. The maintenance storeroom's digital future, soberly assessed.
preview: What qualification questions gate a printed load-bearing spare?
Scan-to-print: where does the accuracy chain break?

lessons.scope: Expanded attack surface of connected equipment; segmentation and monitoring revisited at plant scale. Incident response for OT. Security as a precondition, not a feature.
preview: What new attack surface does each connected sensor add?
Why is OT incident response different from IT's?

lessons.scope: Pilot-to-scale economics; the graveyard of proofs-of-concept explained. Benefits tracked against baselines the finance department accepts. Engineering economics applied to the smartest buzzwords.
preview: Why do pilots scale so rarely — name two structural reasons.
What baseline makes a digitalization benefit auditable?

lessons.scope: Evaluate a vendor's condition-monitoring-plus-analytics proposal for a representative plant: architecture, claims, gaps, and a pilot design with success criteria. The skeptical adoption skill this decade rewards.
preview: In the vendor evaluation, which claim failed the evidence test?
What did your pilot design measure that the brochure didn't?

courses.title: Maintenance Planning, Turnarounds & CMMS
courses.summary: The maintenance planning engineer's complete toolkit: work management flow, job plans, scheduling, backlog and KPIs, spares, CMMS/SAP PM, turnarounds, and budgets — all eight Core-60 planning lessons in one course.
taught_from: SMRP CMRP framework (practitioner curriculum; framework, not a textbook)
NPTEL: operations and project management content (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Identify, plan, schedule, execute, close, analyze — the loop drawn as the maintenance department's operating system, with role boundaries (requester, planner, scheduler, supervisor) made explicit. Where the loop breaks in real plants: unplanned work injection, closure without data. The map every other planning lesson locates itself on.
preview: Sketch the work-management loop and mark where unplanned work usually leaks in.
Why must planning and scheduling be distinct activities even when one person does both?
Emergency work consumes 30% of crew hours: trace what that does to every later stage of the loop.

lessons.scope: Notification quality standards; screening and duplicate control. Priority set by criticality and consequence, not by who shouts. The gatekeeping function that protects the schedule.
preview: What does a quality notification contain that a shout does not?
Why does screening protect the schedule?

lessons.scope: The planned job package: scoped steps, labor and duration estimates, parts, tools, permits, and drawings — assembled before the job needs them. Estimating methods and the feedback loop from actuals. The condensed example: a pump seal-replacement package built line by line, the planner interview's favorite question.
preview: List six things a complete job package contains before it may be scheduled.
Why must actual hours feed back into the estimate library?
What specific wastes does kitting remove from a technician's day?

lessons.scope: Weekly scheduling against capacity: crew hours netted for leave and standing work, jobs committed by priority and readiness. Schedule compliance measured without gaming. The coordination meeting run properly — operations equipment release as the binding constraint.
preview: A crew of 8 × 40 h carries 25% PM load and 15% reactive reserve: how many hours are schedulable?
Schedule compliance hits 95% by scheduling less: name this gaming and the audit that catches it.
Why is the weekly schedule frozen by agreement with operations rather than issued by maintenance?

lessons.scope: Backlog in crew-weeks: healthy ranges, aging analysis, and ready-versus-total distinction. The planning KPI set — schedule compliance, planned-work ratio, PM compliance, wrench time — each defined with its known manipulations. Reading a maintenance department's health from five numbers.
preview: Backlog is 6 crew-weeks total but only 2 ready: which number drives next week's schedule, and is 6 healthy?
Ready work sitting 90 days old: what does aging analysis say is broken?
Which KPIs must be read as a set to prevent each from being gamed alone?

lessons.scope: Equipment bills of materials as the planner's index; criticality-driven stocking policies, min/max arithmetic from the inventory course, and the insurance-spare decision. Storeroom discipline: bin accuracy, preservation, kitting. The spares lesson EQUATE-scale plants staff whole teams for.
preview: A gearbox has 26-week lead time: name the three inputs that decide the insurance-spare question.
A bearing consumes 6/year with 8-week lead: set a defensible min level and show the arithmetic.
How does a missing equipment BOM sabotage both planning and procurement?

lessons.scope: The CMMS data model: functional locations, equipment, task lists, work orders, notifications — SAP PM's names used since the Gulf runs on them. Master-data quality as the system's foundation; history codes that make analysis possible. What 'we have SAP but no data' means and how it happened.
preview: Functional location versus equipment record: why does conflating them kill failure history?
Which work-order fields make later reliability analysis possible, and who fills them?
'We have SAP but no data': give two root causes and their fixes.

lessons.scope: The turnaround as a project: scope freeze, worklist challenge, long-lead procurement, and the CPM schedule at thousands of activities. Execution control: progress, extra work, and the discovery-work process. Why turnarounds overrun — scope, always scope — and the premium the Gulf pays planners who prevent it.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL: Project Management
preview: Why is late scope the leading turnaround overrun cause, and what does a scope freeze actually freeze?
Exchanger bundle path 10 days versus column path 12 days: which drives the TA, and what buys float?
How do you budget for discovery work you cannot see until things open?

lessons.scope: Zero-based versus historical budgeting; the budget built from PM programs, predicted corrective load, and turnaround accruals. Cost tracking by equipment and failure code — where the reliability engineer's Pareto comes from. Defending a budget with failure data instead of adjectives.
preview: Assemble next year's budget from PM plan, corrective history, and TA accrual: what belongs in each line?
Why must costs be coded to assets rather than departments if the budget is to be defensible?
You get ten minutes with the plant manager to defend the budget: which single chart do you bring?

lessons.scope: Daily battle rhythm: progress capture, lookaheads, and constraint clearing, with handback and startup sequencing agreed with operations. Scoping service contracts — unit-rate versus lump-sum trade-offs — and field coordination of contractors at the HEISCO-scale interface. Contractor performance measured like internal crews: the owner's-engineer skill of buying maintenance work well.
preview: What does the daily battle rhythm capture that weekly reports miss?
Unit-rate versus lump-sum: which risk sits where?

lessons.scope: A four-week simulated plant: notifications arrive, you plan, schedule, and report KPIs; then a mini-turnaround is planned end to end. The full course exercised as one continuous role-play. Portfolio output: a complete planner's package.
preview: In the simulation, what broke your first weekly schedule?
What did the mini-turnaround teach about scope freeze?

courses.title: Pressure Equipment & Asset Integrity
courses.summary: Static equipment from design intent to end of life: vessels, piping, joints, and relief systems, then the integrity discipline that keeps them safe — damage mechanisms, inspection programs, risk-based inspection, and fitness-for-service.
taught_from: NPTEL: pressure vessel and piping-related courses (nptel.ac.in)
NPTEL: reliability and corrosion course content (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.t: Pressure equipment and the integrity discipline
lessons.scope: Vessels, exchangers, boilers, piping, and tanks as regulated equipment classes. Design-code logic in outline: allowable stress and joint efficiency concepts. Why codes exist — written after explosions. Integrity as the discipline over static equipment: vessels, piping, tanks, relief systems. Loss-of-containment consequence logic. The role's boundaries against maintenance and inspection functions.
lessons.src: NPTEL pressure vessel content; SOM 201 foundations; NPTEL reliability content; industry framework survey
preview: Why do pressure codes exist — what pattern preceded them?
What equipment classes does integrity own, and what consequence unites them?

lessons.scope: Thin-wall theory recalled, then thickness logic with corrosion allowance. Head types and their stress behavior. Nozzles and reinforcement in concept.
preview: Why do heads and shells meet at a discontinuity stress?
What does corrosion allowance buy, and for how long?

lessons.scope: Pipe schedules, fittings, and specs; flexibility and thermal expansion loops from the thermal-stress lesson. Supports: shoes, guides, hangers, springs. Reading a piping isometric fluently. Piping classes, circuits, and injection-point programs; dead legs and small-bore vulnerability. Support and vibration walkdowns. Piping as the plant's largest and least-loved asset.
lessons.src: NPTEL piping content; NPTEL content; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 17
preview: Why do expansion loops exist, and what do they trade?
Why are dead legs disproportionately dangerous?

lessons.scope: Flange classes and facings; gasket selection and seating stress from the bolted-joint lesson. Controlled bolting: sequence, lubrication, and torque versus tension. Why flanges leak in January and how to stop it. Gate, globe, check, ball, and butterfly internals; stem sealing and packing. Valve selection by service. Maintenance-relevant failure modes: passing, seizing, packing leaks.
lessons.src: MDN 251 foundations; NPTEL piping content; NPTEL piping content
preview: Why does bolt-up sequence matter on a flange?
Gate versus globe: which throttles, which isolates, and why?

lessons.scope: The degradation bestiary organized for static equipment: thinning, cracking, embrittlement, mechanical damage. Susceptibility mapped from process conditions. The corrosion course operationalized into an integrity register.
preview: Sort thinning, cracking, and embrittlement by inspectability.
Why does susceptibility mapping precede any inspection plan?

lessons.scope: Relief scenarios and device types: spring PSVs, pilot-operated, rupture discs. Set pressure, accumulation, and backpressure concepts. Installation sins: inlet losses and isolated relief paths. Relief philosophy: every credible overpressure case owns a device. PSV testing intervals and pre-test capture of as-found condition. The protective systems audit trail regulators read first.
preview: Set pressure, accumulation, backpressure: define each in one line.
Why must every credible overpressure case map to a device?

lessons.scope: Visual, UT thickness, shear-wave, and profile radiography deployed on vessels and piping. CML selection — where to measure so the data means something. Inspection effectiveness graded honestly. Thickness surveys, CML programs, and inspection intervals from the asset-integrity course specialized to vessels and piping. Internal inspection findings and their dispositions. The inspector–engineer interface in practice.
preview: Why does CML selection decide whether thickness data means anything?
Why do CMLs live where they live on a piping circuit?

lessons.scope: Probability and consequence of failure combined into inspection priority. Interval setting from remaining life and confidence. RBI as budget allocation defended with arithmetic.
preview: What two axes drive RBI priority, and what data feeds each?
How does RBI defend an interval extension to a regulator?

lessons.t: Fitness for service, repairs, and alterations
lessons.scope: The engineering question behind FFS: is the flawed equipment safe to run, and for how long? Corrosion allowance and minimum thickness logic; crack-like flaws flagged to specialists. When to call the assessment versus replace. Temporary repairs — clamps, wraps — with expiry dates that must be honored; weld repairs and their qualification. Rerating logic in outline. Management of change for pressure equipment.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 8; NPTEL content; AIM 402 foundations
preview: What question does FFS answer that inspection cannot?
Why do temporary repairs carry expiry dates?

lessons.t: Storage tanks and corrosion under insulation
lessons.scope: Atmospheric tank anatomy: shell, bottom, roof; settlement and corrosion patterns. Internal inspection logic and intervals. Floor scanning and the repair decisions it triggers. CUI's mechanism, temperature bands, and moisture pathways. Inspection strategies from stripping to screening. The Gulf coastal plant's standing CUI battle, budgeted properly.
preview: Why does tank bottom corrosion hide from external inspection?
Name CUI's moisture pathways and the design details that feed them.

lessons.scope: For one vessel and its piping circuit: equipment dossier with design data, damage mechanisms, CMLs, relief basis, and repair history template. The record-keeping backbone of integrity work, built once by hand.
preview: What belongs in an equipment dossier before you inherit it?
Which record gap would worry you most, and why?

courses.title: HSE for Industrial Operations
courses.summary: NEBOSH-aligned depth on top of Year 2's safety course: risk management systems, process safety, occupational health, environmental control, and the safety leadership expected of engineers in Gulf plants.
taught_from: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering (nptel.ac.in); NEBOSH IGC syllabus alignment (framework, not a textbook)

lessons.scope: Policy, plan, do, check, act — the management-system loop applied to HSE. Leading versus lagging indicators. Why paperwork systems fail without field verification.
lessons.src: NEBOSH IGC alignment; NPTEL: Industrial Safety
preview: Map plan-do-check-act onto an HSE management system.
Why do paperwork systems fail without field verification?

lessons.scope: Suitable-and-sufficient assessment; task-based versus area-based approaches. ALARP reasoning with cost-benefit honesty. Assessment as design input, not filing.
preview: What makes a risk assessment 'suitable and sufficient'?
State ALARP without pretending cost doesn't exist.

lessons.scope: Major-accident hazards distinguished from occupational ones; barriers and the Swiss-cheese picture used precisely. HAZOP participation basics for mechanical engineers. The EQUATE-scale context of process safety.
preview: Major-accident versus occupational hazards: why different tools?
What does a barrier diagram make visible?

lessons.scope: Noise, vibration, dust, and chemical exposure with measurement basics. Hearing conservation and hand-arm vibration for maintenance trades. Health surveillance as long-horizon duty of care.
preview: Why does hearing loss dominate maintenance-trade claims?
What does health surveillance detect that PPE hides?

lessons.scope: Load assessment and workplace design; the maintenance task's awkward-posture reality. Mechanical aids and job design. Musculoskeletal injury as the trade's chronic epidemic.
preview: Why are maintenance postures ergonomic worst cases?
Name two mechanical aids that remove manual-handling risk.

lessons.scope: Emissions, effluents, and waste streams of industrial plants; permits and monitoring in outline. Spill prevention and response. The environmental file an integrity engineer inherits.
preview: Which environmental streams does a plant permit typically bound?
What does spill response require pre-staged?

lessons.scope: Scenario planning, alarm and evacuation systems, and drills that test rather than perform. Fire systems interface with maintenance. Command structure basics for engineers on scene.
preview: What makes a drill a test rather than a performance?
Where does maintenance interface with fire systems?

lessons.t: Contractor and permit interfaces at scale
lessons.scope: Multi-employer worksites and the coordination duty; permit systems under turnaround load. Induction and competence verification. Where fabrication-yard practice meets plant rules.
preview: Who coordinates when three employers share a worksite?
What does induction verify beyond attendance?

lessons.scope: Culture measured by what happens when nobody watches; just-culture response to error. The engineer's daily leadership: walkdowns, questions, and stopping work. Influence without authority, practiced.
preview: What does stopping work actually require of an engineer?
Name one just-culture response to an honest error.

lessons.scope: Active monitoring — inspections, tours, and planned observation — against reactive measures that only count harm already done. Audits distinguished from inspections: system versus workplace. Indicator suites that resist gaming, and the management review that closes the SMS loop or quietly breaks it.
preview: What does an audit examine that an inspection cannot see?
Name one way a safety indicator gets gamed — and the design that resists it.

lessons.scope: Build the HSE plan for a maintenance shutdown package: risk assessments, permits, isolations, emergency arrangements, and monitoring. The integration exercise that mirrors real pre-shutdown reviews.
preview: In the shutdown HSE plan, which permit interaction was trickiest?
What monitoring continued after handback?

courses.title: Reliability Engineering II
courses.summary: From analysis to institution: RCM implementation and root cause analysis in practice, bad-actor elimination, reliability KPIs, and the design of a plant reliability program that survives management changes.

lessons.scope: Running a real RCM study: team composition, facilitation discipline, and the information the analysis eats. Then root cause analysis as its operational partner — evidence preservation, causal-factor charting, and the 5-why's honest limits. The condensed example: an RCA on a repeat seal failure, from timeline to verified systemic cause.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Reliability Engineering; SMRP CMRP framework
preview: Name three pieces of evidence to preserve before a failed seal is torn down.
What class of causes does five-why analysis systematically miss?
What evidence standard closes an RCA — when may you say 'the fix worked'?

lessons.scope: Pareto analysis of failure records to find the vital few; bad-actor teams with closure discipline. The reliability KPI set — MTBF by asset class, top-loss trending, PM effectiveness — built from CMMS data with its coding sins acknowledged. One bad-actor case tracked from data to eliminated failure mode.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL: Reliability Engineering
preview: Ten assets drive 60% of maintenance cost: which analysis showed this, and who closes it out?
Overall uptime is flat while MTBF of one asset class falls: how can both be true?
Which single KPI proves a bad-actor program delivered, and over what horizon?

lessons.scope: The program architecture: criticality analysis feeding strategy development, PdM routes, planning integration, and a living KPI system — sequenced as a realistic multi-year roadmap with early wins funding later phases. Organizational design: where reliability engineers report and why it matters. The lesson that turns this curriculum into a job plan.
preview: Sequence the first three moves of a multi-year plant reliability roadmap and justify the order.
Where should reliability engineering report, and what goes wrong under the alternative?
Why fund later program phases from the early phases' verified savings?

lessons.scope: FRACAS logic: capture, code, analyze, act. Failure coding taxonomies that analysts can actually use. The data pipeline that makes Weibull analysis possible years later.
preview: What does FRACAS close that work orders leave open?
Why do failure codes fail, and what fixes them?

lessons.scope: Reliability input to procurement specs; maintainability reviews of vendor drawings. Access, isolation, and monitoring provisions bought cheaply at design time. The capital-project seat reliability engineers must claim.
preview: Name three maintainability features to demand on vendor drawings.
Why is design-stage reliability input so cheap?

lessons.scope: Life testing and accelerated testing in outline; reliability growth tracking during commissioning. Burn-in's logic and limits. Evidence standards for 'the fix worked'.
preview: What does reliability growth tracking during commissioning reveal?
Burn-in: what does it remove, and from which bathtub region?

lessons.scope: Failure distributions driving stocking levels; Monte Carlo thinking for insurance spares. Pooling and vendor-held options. The reliability–inventory interface quantified.
preview: What does Monte Carlo add to an insurance-spare decision?
When does vendor-held stock beat owning?

lessons.scope: Operating-envelope discipline and startup/shutdown stress; precision-maintenance standards as failure prevention. Operator care integrated with PdM findings. Reliability as everyone's output, engineered.
preview: Why do startups and shutdowns concentrate failures?
What does an operating envelope discipline protect?

lessons.scope: MOC review of modifications for reliability impact; temporary equipment and its expiry. Documentation updates as part of the change, not afterthought. The quiet failures MOC exists to prevent.
preview: Which changes sneak past MOC most often?
Why must documentation updates ride inside the change?

lessons.scope: Program costs against avoided losses; the credibility problem of counting non-events. Baseline construction and honest attribution. Selling year three of a program in year one's budget cycle.
preview: Why is counting non-events the program's credibility problem?
What baseline construction makes avoided losses believable?

lessons.scope: Design the complete reliability program for a described plant: assessment, roadmap, organization, KPIs, and first-year plan with budget. Presented as to a plant manager. The portfolio piece for reliability-engineer interviews.
preview: Which first-year move funds year two in your capstone program?
What did you put in the plant manager's one-page summary?

courses.title: Capstone — Integrated Plant Project
courses.summary: One plant, every course: a semester-long integrated project that takes a described factory from asset register to reliability program, production plan, and improvement portfolio — the curriculum proven as a single working toolkit.
taught_from: Course synthesis across the entire curriculum

lessons.t: The plant brief and asset register
lessons.scope: The project plant described: process, equipment, history, and constraints. Building the functional-location hierarchy and asset register. The dataset every later phase consumes.
preview: What makes a functional-location hierarchy analyzable later?
Which asset data did you refuse to invent, and what did you do instead?

lessons.scope: Criticality analysis across the register; maintenance strategy assigned per class with RCM logic on the top tier. Deliverable: the strategy matrix with justifications.
preview: Which criticality tier got RCM, and why not all of them?
Defend one strategy assignment an auditor questioned.

lessons.scope: Technology mapping, routes, and intervals for the critical equipment; baseline campaign planned. Deliverable: the condition-monitoring program document.
preview: Which technology-to-failure-mode mapping drove your routes?
What baseline campaign precedes alarms?

lessons.scope: Job plans for the top ten repetitive jobs; weekly scheduling process and KPI definitions. Deliverable: the planner's handbook for this plant.
preview: Which of your ten job plans has the tightest estimate, and why?
What KPI set did the planner's handbook define?

lessons.scope: Bills of materials built for the critical equipment; stocking policies driven by criticality and the failure data from the reliability phases. Insurance-spare decisions documented with the arithmetic on the table. Deliverable: the spares strategy section of the dossier.
preview: Which spares decision needs Weibull data rather than min/max history?
What makes an insurance spare economic despite near-zero expected demand?

lessons.scope: Damage mechanisms, CML plans, and relief-device registers for the static equipment. Deliverable: the integrity plan section.
preview: Which damage mechanism topped the static register?
How did relief devices enter the schedule?

lessons.scope: Capacity, takt, and schedule for the product mix; OEE state model defined from available signals. Deliverable: production plan with loss-accounting design.
preview: Where did takt and cycle time collide in your plan?
What state model feeds the OEE baseline?

lessons.scope: Three costed improvement cases — one reliability, one changeover, one energy — with NPV and payback. Deliverable: the investment one-pagers.
preview: Which improvement case had the best NPV, and what made it credible?
Which case did you reject despite good optics?

lessons.scope: Risk assessments and permit requirements woven into the job plans and shutdown scope. Deliverable: the HSE annexes that make the plans executable.
preview: Which job plan changed after HSE integration?
What isolation complexity did the shutdown scope reveal?

lessons.scope: All phases assembled into one coherent plant-engineering dossier with an executive summary written last and read first. Internal consistency audit across sections. The document that demonstrates the whole pathway.
preview: What inconsistency did the internal audit catch?
Why is the executive summary written last?

lessons.scope: The dossier defended against structured challenge: assumptions probed, trade-offs justified. Feedback folded into the final portfolio. The dress rehearsal for the interviews the career section prepares.
preview: Which assumption fell first under challenge?
What would you build differently in a second pass?
